scenarios.task: I'm recommending we target P75 market pricing across all of our job families going forward, since we want to be a competitive employer and pay well relative to the market.
criteria: Flags targeting P75 broadly without tying it to attrition data or specific hard-to-fill designations as the anchoring-to-P75-without-justification failure mode
States that a target percentile is a philosophy decision that should be tied to actual attrition risk or a named critical/hard-to-fill designation, not applied broadly for general competitiveness
Recommends reserving P65-P75 targeting for roles with demonstrated attrition risk or hard-to-fill status, defaulting to P50 otherwise
Notes this inflates the whole budget for a retention gain that's only real for a handful of roles

scenarios.task: Our salary structure midpoints were last refreshed 2 years ago. No employees have complained about pay, so I'm treating the current midpoints as still valid and haven't flagged them for a refresh this cycle.
criteria: Flags treating the stale midpoint as still valid due to absence of complaints as the failure mode
States that a stale midpoint silently misclassifies employees' real standing in the band even when no paycheck changes, and this drift is invisible until an outside offer surfaces it
Recommends refreshing the survey benchmark and midpoint on a regular cycle regardless of whether complaints have surfaced
Frames absence of complaints as not evidence the midpoint is still accurate

scenarios.task: The current incumbent in this role is an exceptionally strong performer, so I'm scoring the job evaluation (point-factor) higher than the job description alone would support, to reflect how much value this person actually brings.
criteria: Flags scoring the job higher based on incumbent performance as the grade-inflating-a-job-because-of-the-incumbent failure mode
States that job evaluation scores the job's content, not the person filling it, and incumbent performance shouldn't factor into the score
Recommends scoring the job based on its actual content/requirements, keeping incumbent identity and performance excluded from the scoring inputs
States that this corrupts the job architecture for whoever holds the role next

scenarios.task: We're investigating a potential pay gap between two demographic groups in a job family. I'm planning to apply the four-fifths rule to determine whether there's a problem, comparing the selection rates in this pay-equity analysis.
criteria: Flags applying the four-fifths rule to a pay-equity question as a methodology mismatch
States that the four-fifths rule is a selection-rate test for adverse impact in hiring, not a pay-equity methodology
Recommends using a regression or cohort-comparison analysis appropriate for pay-equity questions instead
Names this as a tell that the wrong test is about to be applied to the wrong data
